did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), a deep learning method, to classify skin cancer. Specifically, it employed a proposed 12-layered CNN model and compared it against five pre-trained models (VGG-16, VGG-19, Inception V3, EfficientNet B7, ResNet 50) using transfer learning on the HAM10000 dataset to achieve skin cancer classification results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a proposed 12-layered CNN model and five pre-trained models (VGG-16, VGG-19, Inception V3, EfficientNet B7, ResNet 50) for skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology, implementation details, and performance metrics (e.g., 90.55% validation accuracy for the proposed model). Although published in the proceedings of the 2nd International Conference on Compting Advancements (ICCA 2022), it reports new empirical findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: An Enhanced CNN Model For Classifying Skin Cancer
journal: Proceedings of the 2nd International Conference on Computing Advancements (ICCA 2022)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of early skin cancer diagnosis by proposing an enhanced 12-layered Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model for classifying seven types of skin lesions. The researchers utilized the HAM10000 dataset, comprising 10,015 dermatoscopic images, and performed preprocessing steps including data augmentation and normalization. The proposed model was trained for 75 epochs using the Ada-max optimizer and compared against five pre-trained models: VGG-16, VGG-19, Inception V3, EfficientNet B7, and ResNet 50. Evaluation metrics included accuracy, loss, precision, and recall, with the proposed model achieving a validation accuracy of 90.55%. The results demonstrate that the custom CNN outperformed the pre-trained transfer learning models, achieving the highest accuracy among all tested architectures.

all_task_types.target: Seven types of skin lesions (Bowen’s disease, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis-like lesions, Dermatofibroma, Melanocytic nevi, Vascular lesions, and Melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin cancer using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) using a proposed 12-layered CNN and five pre-trained models (VGG-16, VGG-19, Inception V3, EfficientNet B7, ResNet 50). The output involves assigning an image to one of the seven classes, with performance evaluated via accuracy, precision, and recall.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermatoscopic images collected from various demographics and archived using various modalities. It is described as an enormous skin cancer dataset with a CC-0 license, serving as the primary source for dermatoscopic image processing studies in this work.

use.training: Used to train the proposed 12-layered CNN model (7500 images) and the five pre-trained models (VGG-16, VGG-19, Inception V3, EfficientNet B7, ResNet 50) via transfer learning.
use.validation: Used for validation to assess model performance (e.g., validation accuracy of 90.55% for the proposed model).
use.testing: Used to test the proposed model (2500 images) and evaluate performance metrics including accuracy, loss, precision, and recall.
use.other: Used for comparison against related works and other pre-trained models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, loss, precision, and recall metrics, and comparisons were made based on these descriptive statistics without statistical significance testing.

ResNet 50.note: Listed as a pre-trained model used in the study, but specific quantitative results (accuracy/loss) are not reported in the provided tables.

HAM10000.notes: The proposed model achieved the highest validation accuracy (90.55%) among all tested models. The paper notes a large gap between training and validation accuracy for the proposed model but states this determines the network is not overfitting. Inception V3 had the highest accuracy (97.63%) among the five pre-trained models in validation, but the proposed model outperformed it in overall classification accuracy (90.55% vs 85.25% training accuracy for Inception V3, though the text highlights the proposed model's superiority). The paper also compares against related works: Dorin Moldovan (DenseNet121) achieved 85% and 75% accuracy in two steps, and another CNN achieved 80% accuracy.

architecture_used.main_model: Proposed 12-layered CNN model; five pre-trained models (VGG-16, VGG-19, Inception V3, EfficientNet B7, ResNet 50)
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning used for the five pre-trained models (VGG-16, VGG-19, Inception V3, EfficientNet B7, ResNet 50). The proposed model was trained from scratch on the HAM10000 dataset.

layer_details.description: Flattens the output from the last convolutional layer

proposed_cnn.dropout: Dropout layer with drop-off rate of 0.3 (reported in Table 1)

preprocessing_techniques: Data cleaning (removing blurry images and images far from interest)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Mentioned as one of the preprocessing steps performed on the HAM10000 dataset, but specific techniques (e.g., rotation, flipping, zoom) or parameters are not reported in the provided text.

conclusions: The proposed 12-layered CNN model achieved a validation accuracy of 90.55%, which was the highest among all tested models, including five pre-trained models (VGG-16, VGG-19, Inception V3, EfficientNet B7, and ResNet 50) and related works.
The proposed model outperformed the pre-trained transfer learning models in terms of classification accuracy for skin cancer detection using the HAM10000 dataset.
The proposed model achieved a precision of 92.89% and a recall of 91.85%.
The authors concluded that the large gap between training accuracy (98.67%) and validation accuracy (90.55%) indicates that the network is not overfitting the dataset.
The study provides a comprehensive comparison and analysis of various deep neural networks for skin cancer categorization.
Future work aims to improve performance metrics, minimize computation time, and expand the model's scope to identify additional types of skin lesions.
Future improvements include utilizing color-constancy approaches and pre-processing techniques to eliminate hair, shades, and glare to more accurately distinguish lesion attributes.
important_remarks: The paper claims the large gap between training accuracy (98.67%) and validation accuracy (90.55%) indicates the network is 'not overfitting'; this is a methodological error, as such a large gap typically indicates significant overfitting or data leakage.
The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have class imbalance and demographic biases, limiting the generalizability of the results to other populations.
There is no external validation performed; the model was only tested on the held-out 25% split of the HAM10000 dataset, raising concerns about real-world clinical applicability.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the comparison between the proposed model and pre-trained models, relying solely on point estimates of accuracy.
Specific data augmentation techniques (e.g., rotation, flipping) and parameters are not detailed, only listed as 'Data Augmentation' in the methodology.

The proposed model uses a very small input size (64x64 pixels), which may discard fine-grained diagnostic features critical for skin cancer classification compared to standard higher-resolution inputs.
The paper does not report per-class metrics (precision/recall/F1 per class), only aggregate metrics, making it difficult to assess performance on minority classes like Melanoma or Bowen's disease.
ResNet 50 is listed as a tested pre-trained model in the text and architecture section, but its specific quantitative results (accuracy/loss) are missing from the reported tables.
The study is published in a conference proceedings (ICCA 2022) with limited page count (4 pages), which may constrain the depth of methodological reporting and ablation studies.
